coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive determination of 4-nitrophenol with coumarin-based fluorescent molecularly imprinted poly (ionic liquid)
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Hao
Deng, Ziyi
Zeng, Yanbo
Zhang, Jian
Yang, Yiwen
Ma, Qinyan
Hu, Wenling
Guo, Longhua
Li, Lei
Wan, Shulin
Liu, Haiying
coredata.dc:description: A coumarin-based fluorescent molecularly imprinted poly (ionic liquid) (FL-MIPIL) was prepared using a new coumarin-based alkenyl fluorescent ionic liquid (coumarin-FL-IL) as the functional monomer, IL [V2C4(mim)2][(PF6)2] and ethyleneglycol dimethacrylate as the cross-linkers, and 4-NP as the template molecule. The absolute quantum yields of coumarin-FL-IL and FL-MIPIL were 7.26 % and 30.66 %, respectively. As a result of the electron transfer between coumarin-FL-IL which contains amino groups and 4-NP bearing hydroxyl groups, FL-MIPIL fluorescence was effectively quenched by 4-NP. The prepared FL-MIPIL sensor can rapidly respond to 4-NP within 60 s. The FL-MIPIL sensor had good linear response to 4-NP from 0.001–7.5 μM and low detection limit of 0.5 nM (S/N = 3). The FL-MIPIL sensor exhibited high sensitivity and good selectivity for 4-NP. The outstanding performance of FL-MIPIL could be ascribed to high fluorescence intensity of FL-MIPIL without matrixes and more interactions between FL-MIPIL and 4-NP. The FL-MIPIL sensor has successfully applied to the determination of 4-NP in lake, rain and waste water samples, river sediment, soil and urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymer
Poly (ionic liquid)
4-Nitrophenol
Coumarin
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Condition optimization on fluorescence efficiency of FL-MIPIL

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of coumarin-FL-IL, EGDMA, [V2C4(mim)2][(PF6)2], FL-EGDMA-MIP, FL-NIPIL and FL-MIPIL.
SEM images of FL-MIPIL (A) and FL-NIPIL (B). Nitrogen-adsorption-desorption isotherm (C) and pore size distribution (D) of FL-MIPIL and FL-NIPIL.
The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of compound c and coumarin-FL-IL (each at a concentration of 15 μM).

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of FL-MIPIL and FL-MIPIL with 0 and 5 μM 4-NP. (B) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of FL-MIPIL. The absorption spectra of FL-MIPIL, Coumarin-FL-IL (30 μM), 4-NP (40 μM), and Coumarin-FL-IL (30 μM) in presence of 4-NP (40 μM) in phosphate buffer (pH 8.0, 0.1 M). Schematic diagrams of FL-MIPIL fluorescence quenching mechanism (C) and the molecular orbital theory (D).

The influence of FL-MIPIL concentration (A) and pH (B) on fluorescence quenching value (ΔF) at FL-MIPIL for 5 μM 4-NP. (C) The fluorescence emission spectra of FL-MIPIL for 5 μM 4-NP at different response time. The influence of response time (D) on fluorescence quenching value (ΔF) at FL-MIPIL for 5 μM 4-NP.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of FL-MIPIL with increasing 4-NP concentrations. (B) Linear correlation between quenching efficiency of FL-MIPIL, FL-EGDMA-MIP, FL-NIPIL and FL-EGDMA-NIP and 4-NP concentrations.

(A) Quenching efficiency of FL-MIPIL for 5 μM 4-NP and its analogues. (B) Quenching efficiency of FL-MIPIL for 5 μM 4-NP and 4-NP in presence of analogues. (C) Chemical structures of 4-NP (a), 2-NP (b), 3-NP (c), 4-nitroaniline (d), 3-chlorophenol (e), phenol (f), hydroquinone (g), 4-tert-butylphenol (h), bisphenol A (i), diethylstilbestrol (j), resorcinol (k), aniline (l), 4-anisidine (m), dicyanodiamide (n) and dopamine (o). Confocal images of FL-MIPIL in presence of 0 μM 4-NP (a, D), 5 μM 4-NP (E), 10 μM 4-NP (F), 5 μM d (G), 5 μM g (H), 5 μM i (I), 5 μM 4-NP and 5 μM d (J), 5 μM 4-NP and 5 μM g (K), 5 μM 4-NP and 5 μM i (L).
(A) The preparation route for coumarin-FL-IL. (B) The preparation and detection procedure of FL-MIPIL.
Comparison of this assay with other fluorescence methods for detecting 4-NP.

row.entry: Carbon quantum dots-immobilized zirconium-based metal-organic framework
Molecularly imprinted carbon dot grafted YVO4:Eu3+ (TEOS a as cross-linker)
MIP-coated CdTe quantum dots (MBA b as cross-linker)
MIP-capped CdTe quantum dots (TEOS as cross-linker)

Coumarin-based FL-MIPIL
(IL [V2C4(mim)2][(PF6)2] and EGDMA as cross-linker)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of 4-NP in practical samples using FL-MIPIL sensor (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive determination of 4-nitrophenol with coumarin-based fluorescent molecularly imprinted poly (ionic liquid)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biological, Chemical Sciences and Engineering, Jiaxing University, Jiaxing 314001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Michigan Technological University, 1400 Townsend Drive, Houghton, MI 49931, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Michigan Technological University, 1400 Townsend Drive, Houghton, Michigan 49931, United States)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A coumarin-based fluorescent molecularly imprinted poly (ionic liquid) (FL-MIPIL) was prepared using a new coumarin-based alkenyl fluorescent ionic liquid (coumarin-FL-IL) as the functional monomer, IL [V2C4(mim)2][(PF6)2] and ethyleneglycol dimethacrylate as the cross-linkers, and 4-NP as the template molecule. The absolute quantum yields of coumarin-FL-IL and FL-MIPIL were 7.26 % and 30.66 %, respectively. As a result of the electron transfer between coumarin-FL-IL which contains amino groups and 4-NP bearing hydroxyl groups, FL-MIPIL fluorescence was effectively quenched by 4-NP. The prepared FL-MIPIL sensor can rapidly respond to 4-NP within 60 s. The FL-MIPIL sensor had good linear response to 4-NP from 0.001–7.5 μM and low detection limit of 0.5 nM (S/N = 3). The FL-MIPIL sensor exhibited high sensitivity and good selectivity for 4-NP. The outstanding performance of FL-MIPIL could be ascribed to high fluorescence intensity of FL-MIPIL without matrixes and more interactions between FL-MIPIL and 4-NP. The FL-MIPIL sensor has successfully applied to the determination of 4-NP in lake, rain and waste water samples, river sediment, soil and urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP) as a precursor and intermediates has been extensively used in the manufacture of pesticides, pharmaceuticals and dyes (Han et al., 2019). 4-NP is considered as a harmful contaminant with significant adverse effects on human health. In addition, it leads to significant damage to the environment. The maximum permitted 4-NP concentration in drinking water according to US Environmental Protection Agency is 0.43 μM (Wei et al., 2011). 4-NP has been detected by many analytical methods, such as electrochemical method (Rajkumar et al., 2018), gas chromatography (Pastor-Belda et al., 2018), liquid chromatography (Zhong et al., 2016) and spectrofluorimetry (Shu et al., 2018). Among these methods, fluorescence method is superior to other methods due to simple sample pretreatment, rapid response and low cost (Li et al., 2016).

Molecular imprinting has been considered as a versatile method for preparing synthetic receptors with recognition sites (Luo et al., 2019). Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are utilized to determine targets (BelBruno, 2018). Fluorescent MIPs (FL-MIPs) as a type of MIP have attracted great attention because they can rapidly produce detectable optical signals after binding to target molecules (Yang et al., 2018; Shariati et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019a). These FL-MIPs, combining properties of MIPs (e.g. high selectivity through recognition sites) and fluorescence detection, have been employed to detect various analytes. In general, FL-MIPs can be prepared by two main approaches (Wang et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2016). The first approach is embedding fluorescent signal into MIPs and determining analytes with fluorescence method. The optical materials, such as gold nanoclusters (Shu et al., 2018), carbon quantum dots (QDs) (Ke et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019b), graphene QDs (Mehrzad-Samarin et al., 2017, 2019), CdTe QDs (Yu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018a), CdSe QDs (Liu et al., 2018a), Mn-doped ZnS QDs (Wang et al., 2018b; Chantada-Vázquez et al., 2016) and organic fluorescent compounds (Descalzo et al., 2013) have been used as fluorescent signal sources. In the second approach, FL-MIPs are synthesized using functional monomers with fluorophore, such as allyl fluorescein (Qiu et al., 2017), 1,8-naphthalimide derivatives (Xu et al., 2016; Limaee et al., 2019), zinc(II)–protoporphyrin (Tong et al., 2002), dansyl (Inoue et al., 2013) and pyrenedimethacrylamide (Chen et al., 2014). Recently, an anthracene-based alkenyl ionic liquid has been used as an anthracene-based fluorescent functional monomer to prepare fluorescent molecularly imprinted poly (ionic liquid) (FL-MIPIL), which was applied to highly sensitive determination of 4-nitroaniline (Lu et al., 2018). Compared with traditional functional monomers with fluorophore, FL-MIPIL prepared with alkenyl ionic liquid-based fluorescent functional monomers had electrostatic interaction between monomer and analytes, which may contribute to more fluorescent responses and higher sensitivity for analytes. To our knowledge, there are few reports about use of alkenyl ionic liquid as fluorescent functional monomer to prepare FL-MIPIL.

Poly (ionic liquid)s (PILs) are usually obtained through the polymerization of alkenyl IL monomers. To increase the selectivity of PILs, molecular imprinting is applied in the preparation of MIPILs (Ding et al., 2019). Compared with MIPs on basis of traditional monomers and cross-linkers, such as methacrylic acid and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate, MIPILs had superior electrocatalytic activity owing to good electrical conductivity of IL and higher adsorption ability for the template molecules due to more interactions between ILs and aromatic analytes, including electrostatic and π-π interactions (Zhu et al., 2018; Ma and Row, 2018). The more interactions could be mainly explained that the imidazole group of alkenyl IL exhibited electrostatic and π-π interactions with aromatic analytes (Ma and Row, 2018). MIPILs have been mainly employed in electrochemical sensors (Wang et al., 2018c) and extraction (Fan et al., 2018; Chen and Huang, 2017). However, MIPIL application in fluorescent detection has rarely been reported.

Herein, a new coumarin-based FL-MIPIL was prepared using a coumarin-based alkenyl fluorescent ionic liquid (coumarin-FL-IL) as a functional monomer and then used for fluorescent determination of 4-NP. This new coumarin-FL-IL was synthesized using 7-(diethylamino)-2-oxo-2H-chromene-3-carboxylic acid as the starting reagent. FL-MIPIL was characterized by FT-IR, SEM and Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm. The sensing properties and practical application of FL-MIPIL for detection of 4-NP were studied.

7-(diethylamino)-2-oxo-2H-chromene-3-carboxylic acid (compound a), 3-bromopropylamine hydrobromide, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), 4-chloroaniline, diphenylamine, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 4-NP, 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), 4,4′-diaminobiphenyl, 4-anisidine, 4-nitrobenzyl bromide and nitrobenzene were purchased from Aladdin Corporation. 1-Vinylimidazole was purchased from TCI Co., Ltd. Ethyleneglycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The same method in the literature (Liu et al., 2018b) for synthesizing IL 1,4-butanediyl-3,3’-bis-l-vinylimidazolium dihexafluorophosphate ([V2C4(mim)2][(PF6)2]). Phosphate buffer was prepared by mixing 0.1 M NaH2PO4 with 0.1 M Na2HPO4.

1HNMR and 13CNMR spectroscopy were carried out using nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (Varian, USA). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was performed with JSM-7500 F microscope (JEOL, Japan). Nexus 470 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer was utilized to measure FT-IR spectra (Nicolet, USA). Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms were acquired using a Micromeritics ASAP2020 static volumetric analyzer (Micromeritics, USA). Fluorescence intensity was measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-7000, Japan). The absolute quantum yields were obtained by FLS980 Edinburgh fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) with an integrating sphere. Fluorescence confocal microscopic images were acquired on TCS SP8 confocal microscope with a 405 nm laser light (Leica, Germany).

As described in Scheme 1 A, coumarin-FL-IL was prepared according to the reported literatures with a minor modification (Gustafson et al., 2012; Yuan and Antonietti, 2011). The synthesized process of coumarin-FL-IL is included in supplementary material.

Scheme 1B shows the preparation procedure of FL-MIPIL. 4-NP (0.1 mmol), coumarin-FL-IL (0.1 mmol) and acrylamide (0.3 mmol) were thoroughly mixed with 30 mL of mixed solvent (methanol : acetonitrile = 1:1) for 15 min. EGDMA (1 mmol), IL [V2C4(mim)2][(PF6)2] (1 mmol) and AIBN (50 mg) were added. After treated with nitrogen for 0.5 h, the solution was incubated at 60 ℃ for 24 h. The solid product was centrifuged and washed with methanol-acetic acid solution (9:1, v/v) to get rid of t 4-NP using soxhlet extraction method. The obtained product was then dried under vacuum overnight to obtain FL-MIPIL. Finally, the resultant FL-MIPIL was ground into powder for further use. Non-imprinted polymer ionic liquid (FL-NIPIL) was synthesized on basis of the same procedure but without the template 4-NP. For comparison, other samples, including FL-MIP with only traditional cross-linker EGDMA-based polymer, FL-EGDMA-MIP and FL-EGDMA-NIP, were also prepared by the same procedure of FL-MIPIL and FL-NIPIL, replacing EGDMA (1 mmol) and IL [V2C4(mim)2][(PF6)2] (1 mmol) by EGDMA (2 mmol), respectively.

Stock solution (2.0 mg mL−1) of FL-MIPIL (FL-NIPIL, FL-EGDMA-MIP, or FL-EGDMA-NIP) was obtained by dispersing 200 mg of FL-MIPIL (FL-NIPIL, FL-EGDMA-MIP, or FL-EGDMA-NIP) in 100 mL deionized water (18 MΩ cm). 1 mL of FL-MIPIL stock solution (2.0 mg mL−1), 2 mL of phosphate buffer (pH = 8.0, 0.1 M) and 4-NP standard solution was successively added into 10-mL graduated tube. The solution volume was then adjusted to 10 mL with deionized water. Fluorescence intensity was measured at λex/λem = 420 nm/461 nm with a photomultiplier tube voltage of 600 V after 60 s equilibration. ΔF values were calculated by the equation: ΔF = F0− F; where F0 is fluorescence intensity at 461 nm of the polymer (FL-MIPIL, FL-NIPIL, FL-EGDMA-MIP, or FL-EGDMA-NIP) without 4-NP and F is fluorescence intensity at 461 nm of the polymer with 4-NP. All measurements were performed three times at ambient temperature. The measurement of fluorescence confocal microscope for the test solution was as follows. The test solution of FL-MIPIL solution in presence and absence of 4-NP (or 4-NP analogues) was prepared with the same method as described above. A drop of the test solution was dropped onto the glass substrates. The fluorescent emission from 415 to 515 nm of FL-MIPIL onto the glass substrates was observed under a 405 nm laser light.

Lake and rain water samples were obtained from Jiaxing University. Waste water samples before treatment were collected from a municipal sewage treatment plant in Jiaxing city. River sediment samples were collected from Changxian river in Jiaxing city. Soil samples were collected at Yongkang Road in Haiyan county of Jiaxing city. Lake, rain and waste water samples were treated with 0.45 μm filtration membrane without other pretreatment. The treatment of river sediment and soil samples was performed according to a reported procedure (Ma et al., 2019). After the river sediment and soil samples were naturally dried, the samples were grinded using a mortar and pestle. Then 6.0 g sample was extracted using 24 mL acetone through ultrasonication for 20 min. The mixture was centrifuged for 10 min to obtain the supernatant. The supernatant was dried under nitrogen. The residual was dissolved in 15 mL deionized water for assay. Human urine samples were obtained from healthy laboratory volunteers. All samples were subjected to a 100-fold dilution before assay after filtrated with 0.45 μm filtration membrane (Li et al., 2016). Subsequently, the pretreated water samples were spiked with various concentrations of 4-NP (0, 0.05, 0.1 and 0.5 μM). The fluorescence spectra and intensity of spiked samples were obtained by the same method described above (the part of 2.4 Fluorescence measurement).

Fig. 1 shows FTIR spectra of coumarin-FL-IL, EGDMA, [V2C4(mim)2][(PF6)2] FL-EGDMA-MIP, FL-NIPIL and FL-MIPIL. Coumarin-FL-IL exhibited a peak of 1659 cm−1 owing to CO vibration of amide group. EGDMA peaks of around 1723 and 1155 cm-1 were assigned to CO and CO vibration, respectively. The spectra of [V2C4(mim)2][(PF6)2] showed the characteristic peak of 843 cm-1 attributing to the PF vibration of PF6- (Nie et al., 2015). The above characteristic peaks of coumarin-FL-IL, EGDMA and [V2C4(mim)2][(PF6)2] were observed in the spectra of FL-MIPIL, suggesting that FL-MIPIL was successfully synthesized. FL-NIPIL exhibited similar peaks with FL-MIPIL. FL-EGDMA-MIP had the characteristic peaks of EGDMA without the characteristic peak of PF6- (843 cm−1). These results further confirmed the successful synthesis of FL-MIPIL. The SEM images (Fig. 2 A and B) showed that the morphology of FL-MIPIL and FL-NIPIL were obviously different. Compared with FL-NIPIL, FL-MIPIL had a porous and rough structure, which is beneficial to adsorption for 4-NP.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm of FL-MIPIL and FL-NIPIL are presented in Fig. 2C and D. The specific surface areas of FL-MIPIL and FL-NIPIL were 68.22 and 5.31 m2 g−1, respectively. The FL-MIPIL surface area was 12.8 times higher than that of FL-NIPIL, which further supported the SEM results showing that FL-MIPIL had a porous structure. The pore size distribution curve (Fig. 2D) shows that the average pore width of FL-MIPIL and FL-NIPIL were 17.90 nm and 6.01 nm, respectively. Higher specific surface area and average pore width of FL-MIPIL can provide more binding sites to the target molecules.

As shown in Fig. 3 , we further investigated the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of compound c and coumarin-FL-IL (each at a concentration of 15 μM). The maximum excitation wavelengths of compound c and coumarin-FL-IL were around 415 nm and 420 nm, respectively. In addition, the maximum emission wavelengths of compound c and coumarin-FL-IL were around 458 nm and 461 nm, respectively. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of coumarin-FL-IL was 2.1 times higher than that of organic compound c under each maximum excitation wavelength (Fig. 3). The enhanced fluorescence intensity may be due to that coumarin-FL-IL contains electron-donating imidazole groups with amino groups compared with compound c (Orita et al., 2018). Furthermore, alkenyl groups of coumarin-FL-IL provided the basic condition of free radical polymerization. Therefore, it can be inferred that coumarin-FL-IL is an ideal fluorescent functional monomer.

The absolute quantum yields of coumarin-FL-IL and FL-MIPIL were 7.26 % and 30.66 % using the absolute method (Liu et al., 2017), respectively. The higher quantum yield of FL-MIPIL could be attributed to the synergetic effect of coumarin-based IL functionalized into FL-MIPIL framework. As presented in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence emission spectra of FL-MIPIL in absence and presence of 4-NP were obviously different. Fluorescent intensity of FL-MIPIL in the presence of 4-NP was relatively weak. The fluorescence quenching could be attributed to the electron transfer between FL-MIPIL and 4-NP, which was consistent with the reported literatures (Yu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018b; Tu et al., 2008; Liu et al., 2010). The FL-MIPIL with amino groups binds to 4-NP with hydroxyl groups using hydrogen bonding interactions. 4-NP accepts the electron from the photoexcited FL-MIPIL and quenches its fluorescence (Fig. 4C). This interaction between coumarin-FL-IL as function monomer of FL-MIPIL and 4-NP as template molecule can be confirmed from the UV–vis spectra. Fig. 4B shows the UV–vis spectra of coumarin-FL-IL solution in presence and absence of 4-NP. The great difference of the two spectra indicates that there is a strong interaction between coumarin-FL-IL and 4-NP (Liu et al., 2010).

In addition, the phenomenon of the fluorescence quenching could be explained by the molecular orbital theory (Fig. 4D). The electrons of FL-MIPIL were excited from the valence band (the ground state) to the conduction band and transited to the initial condition to produce the blue emission (pathway a in Fig. 4D). From the absorption spectra of coumarin-FL-IL and 4-NP (Fig. 4B), it can be found that the UV absorption band of the 4-NP (at 226 nm) is near to the band gap of FL-MIPIL. The electrons at the conductive band of FL-MIPIL can directly transfer to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of UV band of 4-NP (pathway b in Fig. 4D) (Yu et al., 2017). Moreover, the wavelengths of visible absorption of 4-NP (at above 400 nm) are shorter than the emission wavelength of FL-MIPIL (at 461 nm) (Fig. 4B). Therefore, the electrons may also transfer from the conductive band of FL-MIPIL band to the LUMO of the visible band of 4-NP (pathway c in Fig. 4D). Because the energy bands of 4-NP are higher than the blue emission of FL-MIPIL (at 461 nm), the excited electrons are apt to return to the ground state, which result in fluorescence quenching of FL-MIPIL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization on fluorescence efficiency of FL-MIPIL
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optimal conditions for fluorescence detection, several parameters affecting the detection efficiency were optimized in the presence of 5 μM 4-NP. When FL-MIPIL concentration was increased from 0.1–2.0 mg mL−1, fluorescence quenching value ΔF obviously increased due to that the fluorescence efficiency of FL-MIPIL was enhanced (Fig. 5 A). On the other hand, when FL-MIPIL concentration was increased to above 2.0 mg mL−1, ΔF decreased. Thus we selected 0.2 mg mL-1 FL-MIPIL concentration as optimal concentration. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, ΔF increased with increasing pH from 4.0–8.0. 4-NP exhibits in the form of molecule with a pKa 7.1 under acidic conditions (Ma et al., 2019). Under alkaline condition, the oxygen anion of 4-NP was formed with the increase of conjugated degree (Han et al., 2019). It is likely due to the highest binding interaction between 4-NP and FL-MIPIL at pH 8.0. By contrast, when pH was from 8.0–11.0, ΔF reduced, which was attributed to the decreased binding interaction above pH 8.0. Based on this result, pH 8.0 was optimum pH. To assess the accessibility of binding sites, the response time of FL-MIPIL for 4-NP was investigated (Fig. 5C and D). We found that ΔF increased rapidly from 10 to 60 s and attained a plateau above 60 s, suggesting the response equilibrium of FL-MIPIL for 4-NP. This short response time could be attributed to porous structure of FL-MIPIL, which can offer a high mass transfer rate and sites for binding 4-NP. Hence, 60 s was selected as optimum response time.

The fluorescence intensity of FL-MIPIL was gradually quenched with increasing 4-NP concentration (Fig. 6 A). The quenching effect of 4-NP can be calculated by the Stern-Volmer equation (Chen and Rosenzweig, 2002):
F0/F = 1+ K sv C
Where C is 4-NP concentration, and Ksv is the quenching constant of 4-NP. The FL-MIPIL sensor had good linear response to 4-NP from 0.001–7.5 μM with detection limit of 0.5 nM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 6B), which is lower than 0.45 μM of the maximum 4-NP concentration allowed in drinking water set by the US Environmental Protection Agency (Wei et al., 2011). The linear regression equation was (F0/F)-1 = 0.58 C (μM) + 0.29 (R2 = 0.9919). To further demonstrate the advantage of FL-MIPIL, the linearity of FL-EGDMA-MIP, FL-NIPIL and FL-EGDMA-NIP in detecting 4-NP were determined and compared to FL-MIPIL (Fig. 6B). The results indicated that FL-MIPIL sensor had higher fluorescence quenching responses and sensitivity for 4-NP than FL-EGDMA-MIP, FL-NIPIL and FL-EGDMA-NIP sensors. Compared with FL-EGDMA-MIP prepared using only the traditional EGDMA cross-linker, FL-MIPIL containing some part of IL [V2C4(mim)2][(PF6)2] had more interactions between IL and 4-NP, which included electrostatic and π-π interactions. The more interactions could be attributed to imidazole group of IL and 4-NP. These interactions contributed to higher fluorescence quenching responses and sensitivity of FL-MIPIL for 4-NP. The lower sensitivity of FL-NIPIL and FL-EGDMA-NIP could be due to no specific recognition sites in NIPs. We also compared the performance of FL-MIPIL with previous fluorescence methods for detecting 4-NP (Table 1 ). Compared with the 4-NP detection by quantum dot-based probes and quantum dot-based MIPs (Table 1) (Han et al., 2019; Shu et al., 2018; Li et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2019; Dang et al., 2019; Hao et al., 2016; Chatzimarkou et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2016), coumarin FL-MIPIL has the highest sensitivity, indicating that FL-MIPIL is superior to these fluorescence detection methods. The highest sensitivity of FL-MIPIL could arise from high fluorescence intensity of PIL-based MIP with electron-donating imidazole groups, and more interactions between IL and 4-NP.

To examine the selectivity of the FL-MIPIL sensor, the fluorescence quenching was monitored with 4-NP and its analogues (2-NP, 3-NP, 4-nitroaniline, 3-chlorophenol, phenol, etc.), each at a concentration of 5 μM (Fig. 7 A). Fig. 7C displayed chemical structures of 4-NP and analogues. The fluorescence quenching was highest for 4-NP and low with 4-NP analogues (Fig. 7A). In addition, we found no interferences for 4-NP detection (change of (F0/F)-1 was < 5%) in the presence of 1-fold 2-NP, 2-fold 3-NP, 5-fold 4-nitroaniline, 50-fold 3-chlorophenol, and 100-fold other analogues (Fig. 7B), respectively. Good selectivity of FL-MIPIL for 4-NP is attributed to the imprinted 4-NP sites. FL-MIPIL could recognize 4-NP, resulting in significant fluorescence quenching.

The selectivity for 4-NP (a) of FL-MIPIL was further observed by a laser confocal microscope. The fluorescent emission from 415 to 515 nm of the deposited FL-MIPIL from the glass substrates was observed under 405 nm laser light. The fluorescence intensity obviously reduced in presence of increasing 4-NP concentration (Fig. 7D–F). The fluorescence intensities of FL-MIPIL in presence of 4-NP analogues, including 4-nitroaniline (d), hydroquinone (g) and bisphenol A (i) (5 μM each), were nearly the same (Fig. 7G–I). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of MIPIL in presence of a mixture containing 4-NP and 4-NP analogues (5 μM each) were also almost the same (Fig. 7J–L). These results further demonstrate that FL-MIPIL has high selectivity for 4-NP.

To evaluate its application, the FL-MIPIL was utilized to monitor 4-NP in practical samples, including lake, rain and waste water samples, river sediment, soil and urine samples. 4-NP was not detected by the FL-MIPIL sensor in all samples. The samples were spiked with 4-NP standard solutions to demonstrate the accuracy of this method. The recovery for the spiked samples ranged from 95.6%–107.2% (Table 2 ). The relative standard deviation (RSD) values were from 2.35 % to 5.23 % (Table 2). The results indicated that FL-MIPIL has great potential for monitoring 4-NP in actual samples.

A coumarin-based FL-MIPIL was prepared with a new functional monomer of alkenyl coumarin-FL-IL. The absolute quantum yield of FL-MIPIL was 30.66 %. FL-MIPIL with containing electron-donating imidazole groups exhibited high fluorescence intensity. Compared with the 4-NP detection by FL-EGDMA-MIP, some quantum dot-based materials and quantum dot-based MIPs, coumarin FL-MIPIL has the highest sensitivity, suggesting FL-MIPIL is superior to these fluorescence detection methods. FL-MIPIL was used for highly sensitive fluorescence determination of 4-NP. In addition, FL-MIPIL exhibited good selectivity and rapid response time for 4-NP as short as 60 s. The excellent performance could be attributed to high fluorescence intensity of FL-MIPIL, more interactions between FL-MIPIL and 4-NP. The work demonstrates the successful use of alkenyl coumarin-based fluorescent IL as the functional monomer for preparing FL-MIPIL. FL-MIPIL has successfully applied to the determination of 4-NP in lake, rain and waste water samples, river sediment, soil and urine samples. Importantly, the work may provide a direction of developing new fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymers with selectivity and sensitivity for target analytes through preparing FL-MIPIL on basis of new alkenyl fluorescent IL. It should be pointed out that FL-MIPIL sensor had no interferences for 4-NP detection in the presence of 1-fold 2-NP and 2-fold 3-NP, respectively. Future work will be focused on further increasing the selectivity of FL-MIP for the template molecule in presence of very similar compounds through the introduction of more recognition groups or aptamers.
The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.

Hao Dai: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Ziyi Deng: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Yanbo Zeng: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Jian Zhang: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Yiwen Yang: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Qinyan Ma: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Wenling Hu: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Longhua Guo: Writing - review & editing. Lei Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Shulin Wan: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Haiying Liu: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21507041, 21677060), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant Nos. LY20B050009, LY16B050007 and LQ14B050002, the Program for Science and Technology of Zhejiang (No. LGC19B050007), the Open Project of MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology (No. FS18011).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.122854.
